542	\begin{lemma}
543	
544	\leanok
545	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
546	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
547	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
548	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
549	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
550	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
551	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
552	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
553	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
554	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
555	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
556	\end{lemma}
557	
558	\begin{proof}
559	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
560	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
561	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
562	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
563	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
564	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
565	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
566	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
567	\end{proof}
568	
569	\begin{lemma}
570	[Associativity of $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
571	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
572	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
573	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
574	  % NOTE: no Lean declaration yet; blocked on lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso.
575	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L, M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) line
576	  bundles. Then there exists an isomorphism
577	  \[
578	    (L \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N
579	      \;\cong\;
580	    L \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N)
581	  \]
582	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); equivalently the proposition
583	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((L \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N \cong
584	  L \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N)\bigr)\) holds.
585	\end{lemma}
586	
587	\begin{proof}
588	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
589	  Choose a common affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\)
590	  trivialising \(L, M, N\) simultaneously. By
591	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} every tensor product
592	  occurring on either side is again a line bundle;
593	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which holds for arbitrary
594	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules) applies and both sides restrict to the
595	  iterated tensor product of \(A_i\)-modules on \(U_i\). There the associator
596	  \((L_i \otimes_{A_i} M_i) \otimes_{A_i} N_i \cong
597	  L_i \otimes_{A_i} (M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i)\) is the standard associativity
598	  isomorphism of the tensor product of modules over a commutative ring. These
599	  local isomorphisms agree on overlaps --- naturality of the module
600	  associator against the restriction-comparison isomorphisms of
601	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and therefore glue to a global
602	  isomorphism. Only the \emph{existence} of the global isomorphism is
603	  asserted; no compatibility with a pentagon identity is claimed or needed.
604	\end{proof}

606	\begin{lemma}
607	[Unit law for $\otimes_X$ on line bundles]
608	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
609	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
610	  % NOTE: no Lean declaration yet; blocked on lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso.
611	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) a line
612	  bundle. Then \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L\) and
613	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong L\); equivalently
614	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X L \cong L)\) holds (and
615	  symmetrically on the right).
616	\end{lemma}
617	
618	\begin{proof}
619	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
620	  On a trivialising affine cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(L\), restriction
621	  commutes with \(\otimes_X\) by \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and the
622	  structure sheaf restricts to \(A_i\) as an \(A_i\)-module; the unitor
623	  \(A_i \otimes_{A_i} L_i \cong L_i\) is the standard left-unit isomorphism of

652	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}

654	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
655	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
656	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
657	  bundle. The dual
658	  \[
659	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
660	  \]
661	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
662	  isomorphism
663	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
664	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
665	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
666	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
667	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
668	\end{lemma}

670	\begin{proof}
671	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
672	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
673	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
674	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
675	  The contraction map
676	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
677	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
678	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
679	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
680	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
681	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
682	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
683	\end{proof}

766	\end{lemma}

768	\begin{proof}
769	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
770	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
771	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
772	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
773	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
774	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
775	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
776	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
777	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
778	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
779	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
780	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
781	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
782	  same cover.
783	\end{proof}
784	
785	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}

789	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean

793	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item four existence-of-isomorphism facts on \emph{line bundles}
80	    (locally-trivial rank-one modules): associativity
81	    \((L \otimes M) \otimes N \cong L \otimes (M \otimes N)\), unit
82	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes L \cong L\), commutativity
83	    \(L \otimes M \cong M \otimes L\), and inverse
84	    \(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) with
85	    \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\).
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
89	objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
90	ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
91	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
92	expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
93	triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
94	each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
95	(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). The global
96	compatibility isomorphism is nonetheless available for \emph{arbitrary}
97	modules \(M, N\), because the relevant restriction is along an \emph{open
98	immersion}: restriction is then computed sectionwise, and the comparison of
99	structure-sheaf rings over the image is the \emph{isomorphism}
100	\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so the base change is extension of scalars along a ring
101	isomorphism --- which is automatically an isomorphism and commutes with
102	\(\otimes\). No line-bundle or flatness hypothesis is needed. This is
103	exactly the carrier on which the relative Picard group law of
104	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} rests, and it is the subject of
105	\cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
106	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
107	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
108	
109	\section{Mathlib API survey}
110	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
111	
112	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
113	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
114	
115	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
116	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
117	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
118	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
119	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
120	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
121	sectionwise tensor product
122	\[
123	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
124	\]
125	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
126	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
127	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
128	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
129	
130	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
131	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
132	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
133	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
134	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
135	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
136	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
137	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
138	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
139	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
140	tensor-product operation
141	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
142	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
143	
144	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
145	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
146	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
147	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
148	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
149	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
150	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
151	\emph{restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
152	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
153	for \emph{arbitrary} modules \(M, N\) and the restriction along an open immersion
154	\(f\), the canonical comparison
155	\((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes N|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
156	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
157	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume. Because \(f\) is an open immersion,
158	restriction is sectionwise and the structure-sheaf comparison is the ring
159	isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\); the comparison is then base change along that
160	ring isomorphism, an isomorphism commuting with \(\otimes\) by
161	\(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\). The sole
162	project-side ingredient is a bounded sectionwise unfolding of
163	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along the open immersion; no
164	flatness and no line-bundle hypothesis enter (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
165	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
166	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
167	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
168	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
169	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
170	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
171	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
172	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
173	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
174	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
175	independently of any upstream timing.
176

133	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
134	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality

137	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),

146	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
147	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
148	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
149	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
150	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
151	\emph{restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
152	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
153	for \emph{arbitrary} modules \(M, N\) and the restriction along an open immersion
154	\(f\), the canonical comparison
155	\((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes N|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
156	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
157	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume. Because \(f\) is an open immersion,
158	restriction is sectionwise and the structure-sheaf comparison is the ring
159	isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\); the comparison is then base change along that
160	ring isomorphism, an isomorphism commuting with \(\otimes\) by
161	\(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\). The sole
162	project-side ingredient is a bounded sectionwise unfolding of
163	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along the open immersion; no
164	flatness and no line-bundle hypothesis enter (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
165	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
166	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
167	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
168	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).

177	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
178	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
179	
180	\begin{definition}
181	
182	\leanok
183	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
184	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
185	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
186	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
187	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
188	  \[
189	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
190	  \]
191	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
192	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
193	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
194	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
195	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
196	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
197	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
198	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
199	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
200	  canonical compatibility
201	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
202	     \;\cong\;
203	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
204	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
205	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
206	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.

200	  canonical compatibility
201	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
202	     \;\cong\;
203	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
204	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
205	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
206	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
207	
208	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
209	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
210	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
211	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
212	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
213	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
214	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
215	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
216	\end{definition}
217	
218	\begin{lemma}
219	
220	\leanok
221	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
222	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
223	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
224	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
225	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
226	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
227	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
228	  \[
229	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
230	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
231	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
232	  \]
233	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
234	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
235	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
236	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
237	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
238	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
239	  \[
240	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
241	    \qquad
242	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
243	  \]
244	  the associator
245	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
246	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
247	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
248	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
249	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
250	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
251	\end{lemma}
252	
253	\begin{proof}
254	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
255	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
256	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
257	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
258	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
259	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
260	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
261	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
262	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
263	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
264	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
265	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
266	\end{proof}
267

346	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}

354	  \[

358	  \]

363	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
364	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and

368	\begin{proof}
369	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map

372	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is

109	\section{Mathlib API survey}
110	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
111	
112	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
113	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
114	
115	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
116	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
117	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
118	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
119	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
120	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
121	sectionwise tensor product
122	\[
123	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
124	\]
125	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
126	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
127	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
128	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
129	
130	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
131	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
132	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
133	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
134	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
135	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
136	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
137	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
138	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
139	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
140	tensor-product operation
141	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
142	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
143	
144	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
145	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
146	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
147	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
148	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
149	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The \emph{single} genuinely
150	non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
151	\emph{restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
152	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
153	for \emph{arbitrary} modules \(M, N\) and the restriction along an open immersion
154	\(f\), the canonical comparison
155	\((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes N|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
156	the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
157	\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume. Because \(f\) is an open immersion,
158	restriction is sectionwise and the structure-sheaf comparison is the ring
159	isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\); the comparison is then base change along that
160	ring isomorphism, an isomorphism commuting with \(\otimes\) by
161	\(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\). The sole
162	project-side ingredient is a bounded sectionwise unfolding of
163	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along the open immersion; no
164	flatness and no line-bundle hypothesis enter (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
165	The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
166	on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
167	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
168	by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
169	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
170	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
171	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
172	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
173	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
174	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
175	independently of any upstream timing.

178	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}

196	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
197	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf

204	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
205	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is

48	
49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67	
68	## Directive Format
69	
70	```markdown
71	# Reference Retriever Directive
72	
73	## Slug
74	<slug>
75	
76	## Topic
77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>
78	
79	## What the dispatcher will use this for
80	<one paragraph: which chapter / strategy decision the source will inform, so I can prioritise which sections to map and which formats to chase>
81	
82	## Seeds (optional)
83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```
99	
100	If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.
101	
102	## What you do
103	
104	1. **Read your directive completely.**
105	
106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.
122	
123	   - **Stacks Project**: fetch the relevant `.tex` chapter verbatim from GitHub raw, so lemma/tag statements are exact (this is the fix for the paraphrase/tag-typo failure mode):
124	     ```bash
125	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>-algebra.tex \
126	       https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/algebra.tex
127	     ```
128	     Record the exact tags the dispatcher needs and confirm each tag's statement against the fetched `.tex`.
129	
130	   - **Journal / book with an open form**: download the open PDF (author homepage, institutional repository, numdam, Project Euclid open, publisher open-access, etc.).
131	
132	   - **General**:
133	     ```bash
134	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf "<url>"
135	     ```
136	     `-f` makes curl fail on HTTP errors instead of silently saving an error page.
137	
138	4. **Verify the download is real**, not a paywall/login/error page wearing a `.pdf` extension:
139	   ```bash
140	   ls -l references/<slug>.pdf
141	   file references/<slug>.pdf      # expect "PDF document", not "HTML document"
142	   ```
143	   A near-empty file, an HTML MIME type, or a "log in to continue" body all mean the fetch FAILED — delete the bad file (`rm references/<slug>.pdf`) and treat that source as not obtained. **Never** rename or keep an unverified download.
144	
145	5. **Write the pointer file** `references/<slug>.md` (format below) in BOTH the success and failure cases:
146	   - **On success:** it points at the real downloaded file(s) and records the exact URL each was retrieved from. It does NOT reproduce the source's content.
147	   - **On failure** (nothing could be downloaded and verified): write the pointer anyway, recording the citation, every URL/channel you tried, and why each failed (paywall, 404, HTML-only, etc.). **Do NOT write the source's mathematical content into it** — a failure pointer carries the attempt log, never a paraphrase or recollection of what the source "probably says."
148	
149	6. **Register the entry** in `references/summary.md`. Append (do not delete existing entries) a one-line index entry referencing the slug.
150	
151	7. **Verify the files are on disk** (`ls -l references/<slug>.*`) before reporting back.
152	
153	## Acquiring paywalled / hard-to-find sources
154	
155	Many sources that look paywalled have a legitimate open copy. Before giving up, try, in order:
156	
157	- **arXiv** for the same title/authors — journal papers very often have an arXiv preprint with identical statements.
158	- **The author's personal / institutional homepage** — search `"<title>" <author> pdf` and `<author> publications`.
159	- **Open repositories**: numdam.org (French journals, seminars — this is where Grothendieck's FGA and Séminaire Bourbaki live), Project Euclid open content, institutional repositories, course pages.
160	- **`filetype:pdf` web searches**: `WebSearch` for `"<exact title>" filetype:pdf`. Some hits open a PDF directly — fetch and verify it.
161	- **Open TeX source**: Stacks Project (GitHub), many lecture notes and books publish LaTeX source openly.
162	
163	**Do NOT** use shadow-library / piracy sites (e.g. Sci-Hub, Library Genesis). Stick to legitimately open copies. If, after these steps, only a paywalled copy exists, fetch whatever open metadata you can (official TOC, abstract) into the pointer file's contents map, mark the source `PAYWALLED — no open copy located`, and say so plainly in your report. Do not paraphrase the body to compensate.
164	
165	## Pointer file format
166	
167	`references/<slug>.md` is an index card, not a summary. Keep it short.
168

233	## `references/summary.md` registration
234	
235	Append one row to the `## File inventory` table. The template has three columns: `File`, `Description`, `How to read (confirmed working)`. Make the retrieval outcome visible in the index itself, so a planner skimming `summary.md` sees at a glance which sources actually have a local file AND which tool they should use to ingest each one:
236	
237	```markdown
238	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → `<slug>.pdf` / `<slug>.tex` | <one-line topic note> | `Read` (with `pages: "1-5"` if long); fallback `pdftotext <slug>.pdf -` |
239	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → ⚠ NOT RETRIEVED (<short reason>) | <topic note> | n/a — no local file |
240	```
241	
242	For the `How to read` column, record what *actually worked* during your verification step (you opened the file to verify it isn't an HTML-paywall stub — write down the command you used). `Read` for PDFs assumes ``poppler-utils`` is installed (``archon setup`` handles it); if `Read` errors with a missing-`pdftoppm` message, record the exact `pdftotext` (or other) fallback you used. The column is a living log — later agents who ingest the same file should overwrite the entry with a better entry if they find one.
243	
244	If `references/summary.md` doesn't exist, create it with the standard header + the inventory table before appending.
245	
246	## Rules
247	
248	### What you CAN do
249	- Use `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` to discover and inspect sources.
250	- Use `Bash` to download, unpack, and verify source files. **Use `curl` — it is present on every supported platform, so no extra install is needed.** `wget` is an optional fallback only if `curl` is somehow missing (`command -v curl || command -v wget`); never assume `wget` exists. `tar`, `file`, and `ls` are likewise standard.
251	- Write downloaded files and the pointer `.md` under `references/`.
252	- Append to `references/summary.md`.
253	
254	### What you MUST do
255	- **Download the real file and verify it.** A pointer file is only valid once its named source file exists on disk and `file` confirms it is the real document.
256	- **Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when openly available, and keep the PDF too.** TeX gives exact statements; the PDF is the universal fallback.
257	- **Cite exact section / page numbers** in the contents map. A map that just says "the paper discusses X" is useless.
258	- **Quote nothing in the pointer file.** Planners quote from the downloaded source, not from you. Your contents map only tells them where to look.
259	
260	### What you MUST NOT do
261	- **Do NOT fabricate or paraphrase the source's content.** No "I believe the paper proves…", no reconstructed proofs in the `.md`. You fetched the file or you didn't.
262	- **Do NOT use piracy / shadow-library sites.** Legitimate open copies only.
263	- **Do NOT delete or rewrite existing `references/` entries.** Only add new ones. If an existing entry is wrong, report it in "Notes for Dispatcher" — do not modify it.
264	- **Do NOT write outside `references/`.**
265	- **Do NOT spawn child subagents.** You are a leaf.
266	
267	## Report format
268	
269	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
270	
271	```markdown
272	# Reference Retriever Report
273	
274	## Slug
275	<slug>
276	
277	## Status
278	<COMPLETE | NOT_FOUND | PARTIAL>
279	<NOT_FOUND: no legitimate open copy of any source could be downloaded.
280	PARTIAL: some sources/formats obtained, others not (e.g. PDF but no TeX, or paywalled).
281	COMPLETE: all directive items downloaded and verified.>
282	
283	## Sources fetched
284	
285	For each: title, URL(s) used, formats downloaded, verification result, pointer file written.
286	
287	- "Some Paper" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1234.5678 — downloaded `references/some-paper.pdf` (PDF, verified) + `references/some-paper.tar.gz` (TeX source, verified) — pointer `references/some-paper.md`.
288	- "Other Book Ch. 5" — paywalled, no open copy located — TOC-only pointer written, NO source file.
289	
290	## Index updates
291	- `references/summary.md` — appended <N> entries: `some-paper`, ...
292	
293	## Notes for Dispatcher
294	<- any source the directive named that turned out to be wrong / mislocated, with the corrected pointer
295	- any related source you noticed that the dispatcher might want next
296	- any pre-existing references/ entry you noticed was wrong or stale (DO NOT modify — report only)
297	- any source you could only obtain paywalled, so the dispatcher can decide whether to find another route>
298	```
299	
300	## Return value
301	
302	Your final assistant message:
303	
304	- One line: `<slug>: <status> — <N> sources downloaded (<formats>), <M> pointer files written`
305	- The path to your full report.
306

Dispatchable by the plan agent and by blueprint-writers when mathematical content needs a source not yet in references/.
4	write_domain: "references/**"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch me whenever a strategic decision or a chapter you are
10	    about to write needs material not already in references/. Calling
11	    me is far cheaper than letting a writer or planner hallucinate
12	    sources or paper a hole with vague prose.
13	
14	  - I fetch the ORIGINAL source FILE (PDF, and TeX/LaTeX source when
15	    it is openly available), not a paraphrase. The companion .md I
16	    write next to it is only a citation pointer + table of contents,
17	    NOT a summary you can quote from. When you cite, open the actual
18	    downloaded source file (e.g. references/<slug>.pdf) and quote it
19	    verbatim — that is the whole point of having me fetch it.
20	
21	  - Plan agent: dispatch me BEFORE writing STRATEGY.md or composing
22	    blueprint-writer directives whenever the iteration touches a
23	    topic not represented in references/summary.md. Treat me as
24	    cheap source preparation, not a last-resort lookup.
25	
26	  - Blueprint-writer: when drafting reveals you need a source you
27	    don't have, dispatch me mid-session and wait for me to return
28	    before writing the affected chapter section. Note that to spawn
29	    me from inside a writer round, the planner must have declared
30	    `references/**` in your --write-domain at writer dispatch time
31	    (see blueprint-writer dispatcher_notes).
32	
33	  - I am NEVER dispatched by read-only subagents (blueprint-reviewer,
34	    lean-auditor, lean-vs-blueprint-checker). Those report what they
35	    see; they do not procure new sources.
36	
37	  - I do NOT fabricate. If the original file genuinely cannot be
38	    located through any legitimate open channel, I report "not found"
39	    rather than guessing content or writing a paraphrase in its place.
40	    Treat my "not found" as authoritative — do not redispatch with
41	    more aggressive prompting; instead reconsider the strategy.
42	
43	  - I download the source under references/<slug>.{pdf,tex,...} and
44	    write ONE pointer file references/<slug>.md registering it, then
45	    add it to references/summary.md. I do not delete or rewrite
46	    existing reference entries.
47	---
48	
49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67	
68	## Directive Format
69	
70	```markdown
71	# Reference Retriever Directive
72	
73	## Slug
74	<slug>
75	
76	## Topic
77	<one or two sentences naming the mathematical area + the specific question>
78	
79	## What the dispatcher will use this for
80	<one paragraph: which chapter / strategy decision the source will inform, so I can prioritise which sections to map and which formats to chase>
81	
82	## Seeds (optional)
83	- arXiv: 1234.5678
84	- DOI: 10.xxxx/yyyy
85	- Title + author: "Some Paper" by Smith, year
86	- URL: https://...
87	- Search query: "<terms the dispatcher recommends>"
88	
89	(If no seeds are given, I search for the topic on arXiv + Google + Stacks Project + nLab as appropriate to the area.)
90	
91	## Out of scope
92	<sources or angles the dispatcher does NOT want — e.g. "no philosophy-of-math sources", "skip historical surveys, want technical content only">
93	
94	## Contents-map depth expected
95	- shallow: top-level table of contents only
96	- medium: section/chapter map with the relevant section(s) flagged + page numbers (default)
97	- deep: also map the specific theorems/lemmas the dispatcher named, with their exact location (section + page) inside the downloaded file — but still NO paraphrase of their content
98	```
99	
100	If the directive omits "Contents-map depth expected", default to **medium**.
101	
102	## What you do
103	
104	1. **Read your directive completely.**
105	
106	2. **Locate the source.**
107	   - If seeds are given, resolve them first.
108	   - Otherwise, `WebSearch` with focused queries derived from the topic. Prefer:
109	     - arXiv for recent technical papers.
110	     - Stacks Project (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu) for algebraic geometry — its TeX source is open on GitHub.
111	     - nLab (https://ncatlab.org) for category-theoretic content.
112	     - Open-access textbooks and lecture notes when the topic is foundational.
113	
114	3. **Download the original file(s)** with `Bash`. Prefer machine-readable source and keep the PDF too:
115	
116	   - **arXiv**: fetch BOTH the LaTeX e-print and the PDF.
117	     ```bash
118	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.tar.gz https://arxiv.org/e-print/<id>
119	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf     https://arxiv.org/pdf/<id>
120	     ```
121	     If the e-print is a single `.tex`, name it `references/<slug>.tex` instead. You may unpack the tarball (`tar xzf`) into `references/<slug>-src/` if it helps map the contents — but keep the original archive too.
122	
123	   - **Stacks Project**: fetch the relevant `.tex` chapter verbatim from GitHub raw, so lemma/tag statements are exact (this is the fix for the paraphrase/tag-typo failure mode):
124	     ```bash
125	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>-algebra.tex \
126	       https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stacks/stacks-project/master/algebra.tex
127	     ```
128	     Record the exact tags the dispatcher needs and confirm each tag's statement against the fetched `.tex`.
129	
130	   - **Journal / book with an open form**: download the open PDF (author homepage, institutional repository, numdam, Project Euclid open, publisher open-access, etc.).
131	
132	   - **General**:
133	     ```bash
134	     curl -fSL -o references/<slug>.pdf "<url>"
135	     ```
136	     `-f` makes curl fail on HTTP errors instead of silently saving an error page.
137	
138	4. **Verify the download is real**, not a paywall/login/error page wearing a `.pdf` extension:
139	   ```bash
140	   ls -l references/<slug>.pdf
141	   file references/<slug>.pdf      # expect "PDF document", not "HTML document"
142	   ```
143	   A near-empty file, an HTML MIME type, or a "log in to continue" body all mean the fetch FAILED — delete the bad file (`rm references/<slug>.pdf`) and treat that source as not obtained. **Never** rename or keep an unverified download.
144	
145	5. **Write the pointer file** `references/<slug>.md` (format below) in BOTH the success and failure cases:
146	   - **On success:** it points at the real downloaded file(s) and records the exact URL each was retrieved from. It does NOT reproduce the source's content.
147	   - **On failure** (nothing could be downloaded and verified): write the pointer anyway, recording the citation, every URL/channel you tried, and why each failed (paywall, 404, HTML-only, etc.). **Do NOT write the source's mathematical content into it** — a failure pointer carries the attempt log, never a paraphrase or recollection of what the source "probably says."
148	
149	6. **Register the entry** in `references/summary.md`. Append (do not delete existing entries) a one-line index entry referencing the slug.
150	
151	7. **Verify the files are on disk** (`ls -l references/<slug>.*`) before reporting back.
152	
153	## Acquiring paywalled / hard-to-find sources
154	
155	Many sources that look paywalled have a legitimate open copy. Before giving up, try, in order:
156	
157	- **arXiv** for the same title/authors — journal papers very often have an arXiv preprint with identical statements.
158	- **The author's personal / institutional homepage** — search `"<title>" <author> pdf` and `<author> publications`.
159	- **Open repositories**: numdam.org (French journals, seminars — this is where Grothendieck's FGA and Séminaire Bourbaki live), Project Euclid open content, institutional repositories, course pages.
160	- **`filetype:pdf` web searches**: `WebSearch` for `"<exact title>" filetype:pdf`. Some hits open a PDF directly — fetch and verify it.
161	- **Open TeX source**: Stacks Project (GitHub), many lecture notes and books publish LaTeX source openly.
162	
163	**Do NOT** use shadow-library / piracy sites (e.g. Sci-Hub, Library Genesis). Stick to legitimately open copies. If, after these steps, only a paywalled copy exists, fetch whatever open metadata you can (official TOC, abstract) into the pointer file's contents map, mark the source `PAYWALLED — no open copy located`, and say so plainly in your report. Do not paraphrase the body to compensate.
164	
165	## Pointer file format
166	
167	`references/<slug>.md` is an index card, not a summary. Keep it short.
168	
169	### Success — file(s) downloaded and verified
170	
171	```markdown
172	# <Title>
173	
174	## Citation
175	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
176	
177	## Slug
178	<slug>
179	
180	## Retrieval status
181	RETRIEVED — <date>
182	
183	## Local source files
184	- references/<slug>.pdf — PDF, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>
185	- references/<slug>.tex — LaTeX source, VERIFIED via `file` — retrieved from <successful URL>   ← if obtained
186	(List every file you saved and the EXACT URL it came from. These successful links are
187	the most important field — a later planner uses them to re-fetch or double-check.)
188	
189	## Why this source
190	<2-3 sentences: which Archon chapter / strategy decision will use this. Copy from the directive's "What the dispatcher will use this for".>
191	
192	## Contents map
193	<A table of contents / section map pointing INTO the downloaded file, so a planner knows where to look. Use the source's own numbering. NO paraphrase of the mathematics — just locations.>
194	
195	- Ch./§ <n> <title> — p.<page>
196	- Ch./§ <n> <title> — p.<page>   ← relevant to this directive
197	- (deep depth only) Theorem/Lemma <number> — §<n>, p.<page>
198	
199	## Caveats
200	<Things the dispatcher should know: paywalled-but-open-copy-found, unusual conventions, known errata, contested results, edition differences. If none, write "None".>
201	
202	## Quality / provenance
203	<one or two sentences: is this the definitive reference or one of several? Where exactly did the file come from, and how confident are you it is the authoritative version?>
204	```
205	
206	### Failure — nothing could be downloaded and verified
207	
208	Still write the pointer, but it is an **attempt log**, not content. A future planner reading it must immediately see that there is no local source to quote and what was already tried (so they don't burn an iteration repeating it).
209	
210	```markdown
211	# <Title>
212	
213	## Citation
214	<author(s)>, "<title>", <venue / publisher>, <year>. <DOI / arXiv ID / URL>.
215	
216	## Slug
217	<slug>
218	
219	## Retrieval status
220	NOT_RETRIEVED — <date>. No local source file exists. DO NOT cite this as `(read from …)`.
221	
222	## Sources tried
223	- <url 1> — <what happened: 404 / paywall / HTML-only login page / corrupt file>
224	- <url 2> — <…>
225	- <search query used> — <no open copy among results>
226	
227	## Why it matters / what to do next
228	<which chapter/decision needed this, and the dispatcher's options: find another route, pick a different source, or treat the dependent block as gated on retrieval>
229	
230	<!-- NO contents map, NO abstract, NO recalled statements. There is nothing verified to record. -->
231	```
232	
233	## `references/summary.md` registration
234	
235	Append one row to the `## File inventory` table. The template has three columns: `File`, `Description`, `How to read (confirmed working)`. Make the retrieval outcome visible in the index itself, so a planner skimming `summary.md` sees at a glance which sources actually have a local file AND which tool they should use to ingest each one:
236	
237	```markdown
238	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → `<slug>.pdf` / `<slug>.tex` | <one-line topic note> | `Read` (with `pages: "1-5"` if long); fallback `pdftotext <slug>.pdf -` |
239	| [`<slug>.md`](./<slug>.md) → ⚠ NOT RETRIEVED (<short reason>) | <topic note> | n/a — no local file |
240	```
241	
242	For the `How to read` column, record what *actually worked* during your verification step (you opened the file to verify it isn't an HTML-paywall stub — write down the command you used). `Read` for PDFs assumes ``poppler-utils`` is installed (``archon setup`` handles it); if `Read` errors with a missing-`pdftoppm` message, record the exact `pdftotext` (or other) fallback you used. The column is a living log — later agents who ingest the same file should overwrite the entry with a better entry if they find one.
243	
244	If `references/summary.md` doesn't exist, create it with the standard header + the inventory table before appending.
245	
246	## Rules
247	
248	### What you CAN do
249	- Use `WebSearch` and `WebFetch` to discover and inspect sources.
250	- Use `Bash` to download, unpack, and verify source files. **Use `curl` — it is present on every supported platform, so no extra install is needed.** `wget` is an optional fallback only if `curl` is somehow missing (`command -v curl || command -v wget`); never assume `wget` exists. `tar`, `file`, and `ls` are likewise standard.
251	- Write downloaded files and the pointer `.md` under `references/`.
252	- Append to `references/summary.md`.
253	
254	### What you MUST do
255	- **Download the real file and verify it.** A pointer file is only valid once its named source file exists on disk and `file` confirms it is the real document.
256	- **Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when openly available, and keep the PDF too.** TeX gives exact statements; the PDF is the universal fallback.
257	- **Cite exact section / page numbers** in the contents map. A map that just says "the paper discusses X" is useless.
258	- **Quote nothing in the pointer file.** Planners quote from the downloaded source, not from you. Your contents map only tells them where to look.
259	
260	### What you MUST NOT do
261	- **Do NOT fabricate or paraphrase the source's content.** No "I believe the paper proves…", no reconstructed proofs in the `.md`. You fetched the file or you didn't.
262	- **Do NOT use piracy / shadow-library sites.** Legitimate open copies only.
263	- **Do NOT delete or rewrite existing `references/` entries.** Only add new ones. If an existing entry is wrong, report it in "Notes for Dispatcher" — do not modify it.
264	- **Do NOT write outside `references/`.**
265	- **Do NOT spawn child subagents.** You are a leaf.
266	
267	## Report format
268	
269	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/reference-retriever-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
270	
271	```markdown
272	# Reference Retriever Report
273	
274	## Slug
275	<slug>
276	
277	## Status
278	<COMPLETE | NOT_FOUND | PARTIAL>
279	<NOT_FOUND: no legitimate open copy of any source could be downloaded.
280	PARTIAL: some sources/formats obtained, others not (e.g. PDF but no TeX, or paywalled).
281	COMPLETE: all directive items downloaded and verified.>
282	
283	## Sources fetched
284	
285	For each: title, URL(s) used, formats downloaded, verification result, pointer file written.
286	
287	- "Some Paper" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1234.5678 — downloaded `references/some-paper.pdf` (PDF, verified) + `references/some-paper.tar.gz` (TeX source, verified) — pointer `references/some-paper.md`.
288	- "Other Book Ch. 5" — paywalled, no open copy located — TOC-only pointer written, NO source file.
289	
290	## Index updates
291	- `references/summary.md` — appended <N> entries: `some-paper`, ...
292	
293	## Notes for Dispatcher
294	<- any source the directive named that turned out to be wrong / mislocated, with the corrected pointer
295	- any related source you noticed that the dispatcher might want next
296	- any pre-existing references/ entry you noticed was wrong or stale (DO NOT modify — report only)
297	- any source you could only obtain paywalled, so the dispatcher can decide whether to find another route>
298	```
299	
300	## Return value
301	
302	Your final assistant message:
303	
304	- One line: `<slug>: <status> — <N> sources downloaded (<formats>), <M> pointer files written`
305	- The path to your full report.
306

At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the dual are present as scheme-level
objects. The four facts of (3) all reduce to a single non-trivial
ingredient --- the \emph{global} compatibility isomorphism
\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso})
expressing that \(\otimes_X\) commutes with restriction to an open. Local
triviality alone does \emph{not} furnish a global associator: two sheaves
each locally isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}\) need not be globally isomorphic
(\(\mathcal{O}(1)\) versus \(\mathcal{O}\) on \(\mathbb{P}^1\)). The global
compatibility isomorphism is nonetheless available for \emph{arbitrary}
modules \(M, N\), because the relevant restriction is along an \emph{open
immersion}: restriction is then computed sectionwise, and the comparison of
structure-sheaf rings over the image is the \emph{isomorphism}
\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so the base change is extension of scalars along a ring
isomorphism --- which is automatically an isomorphism and commutes with
\(\otimes\). No line-bundle or flatness hypothesis is needed. This is
exactly the carrier on which the relative Picard group law of
\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} rests, and it is the subject of
\cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} below. The full symmetric monoidal category on
\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is \emph{not} required; see
\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.

\mathcal{O}_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), exactly Mathlib's

associator, unitors and braiding --- each obtained cheaply as
\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the corresponding presheaf-level
isomorphism, while the inverse is carried by the predicate itself. These
assemble the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is
the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The geometric
compatibility isomorphism \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any separate tensor-inverse lemma are
\emph{off} this critical path. The full symmetric monoidal category on
\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is also \emph{not} required; see
\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.

The \emph{single} genuinely
non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
\emph{restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
for \emph{arbitrary} modules \(M, N\) and the restriction along an open immersion
\(f\), the canonical comparison
\((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes N|_f\) is an isomorphism. This is
the \emph{only} input the four existence-of-iso group-law lemmas of
\cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} consume. Because \(f\) is an open immersion,
restriction is sectionwise and the structure-sheaf comparison is the ring
isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\); the comparison is then base change along that
ring isomorphism, an isomorphism commuting with \(\otimes\) by
\(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\). The sole
project-side ingredient is a bounded sectionwise unfolding of
\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along the open immersion; no
flatness and no line-bundle hypothesis enter (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
The project does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance
on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure: monoidal coherence is never consumed
by a group law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).

The data this operation must supply for the group law are the monoidal
\emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) --- the associator
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the left/right unitors
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) and the braiding
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) --- each obtained by the cheap pattern
\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) applied to the corresponding presheaf-level
isomorphism on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\), with no recourse to the
abstract \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) adjoint. Together with the
\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group
law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The
open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
retained only as an optional supplement.

\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}

The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
critical path.

\begin{definition}[\(\otimes\)-invertibility of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01HK (invertible modules) (verbatim text not yet
  % retrieved --- reference-retriever dispatch required before a verbatim
  % SOURCE QUOTE block may be added here).
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
  \[
    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
  \]
  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
  \(\otimes_X\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01CR (Picard group of a scheme) (verbatim text not yet
  % retrieved --- reference-retriever dispatch required before a verbatim
  % SOURCE QUOTE block may be added here).
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of objects of
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) under the substrate tensor product
  \(\otimes_X\) form a commutative monoid: multiplication is
  \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\), well-defined on isomorphism classes
  because \(\otimes_X\) is a bifunctor
  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, which transports
  \(M \cong M'\) to \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N\) and symmetrically);
  the unit is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and associativity, unitality and
  commutativity are the coherence isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, read as
  equalities of iso-classes. An element \([M]\) is a \emph{unit} of this monoid
  iff \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}): a tensor inverse \(N\) with
  \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) is exactly a multiplicative inverse of
  \([M]\). Hence the group of units of this commutative monoid is the abelian
  group of iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules ---
  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR). This mirrors Mathlib's
  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\)
  and the transported \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), used here as the design
  template; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at
  the pinned commit.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  Well-definedness of \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) on iso-classes is the
  bifunctoriality of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}. The
  commutative-monoid axioms are propositions about iso-classes and are
  discharged by the coherence isomorphisms: associativity by
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws by \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso},
  commutativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}; no pentagon, triangle or
  hexagon identity is invoked, since each axiom asserts only the
  \emph{existence} of an isomorphism. By definition of a unit in a commutative
  monoid, \([M]\) is invertible iff there is \([N]\) with
  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), i.e.\ iff \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). Therefore the units subgroup is
  precisely the iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible modules, and it is an
  abelian group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR). The whole
  assembly follows the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\)
  template.
\end{proof}

\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}

input.old_string:
\emph{Step 3 (sectionwise identification along the open-immersion ring
  isomorphism).} The functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is
  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of pushforward and is consequently
  \emph{opaque} on objects and morphisms: Mathlib provides no sectionwise
  formula for it, and --- unlike the scheme-level restriction --- no
  presheaf-level analogue of \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). The one
  genuinely-new project-side ingredient of this proof is therefore a
  \emph{sectionwise unfolding} of the abstract pullback along the open
  immersion: for the open-immersion ring morphism \(\varphi\), the presheaf
  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,P\) is, section by section over an
  open \(V\), the base change of the sections of \(P\) along the ring
  \emph{isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\) --- that is, restriction of
  sections combined with scalar transport across the isomorphism. (Over an open
  immersion the sections themselves agree definitionally,
  \(\Gamma(M|_f, V) = \Gamma(M, f(V))\) by the \(\mathtt{restrict\_obj}\)
  identity; only the scalar ring is transported, and it is transported along an
  isomorphism.)

  Granting this sectionwise description, the base-change comparison of Step 2
  is, on each section over \(V\), the canonical map of a tensor product under
  base change along the ring isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Base change
  along a ring isomorphism is a \emph{monoidal equivalence} of module
  categories --- it is invertible and carries tensor products to tensor products
  --- by \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\) applied to
  the ring equivalence underlying \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Hence each
  sectionwise comparison is an isomorphism compatible with \(\otimes\). No
  flatness, no \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)-monoidality, and no strong-monoidal
  pullback instance is invoked: the entire force of the step is that the scalar
  change is along an \emph{isomorphism}.

  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise isomorphisms of Step 3 are natural in \(V\)
  (compatible with the presheaf restriction maps, the naturality square being
  that of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) against restriction). They therefore assemble,
  via \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\), into a single presheaf-of-modules
  isomorphism realising the base-change comparison of Step 2 as an iso.
  Transporting this back through the sheafification units of Step 2 and the
  restriction--pullback comparison of Step 1 yields the asserted global
  isomorphism \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\).

  The route uses no monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) or on the
  pullback functor, no flatness, and no line-bundle hypothesis. The single
  bounded project-side ingredient is the sectionwise unfolding of
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along an open immersion (a
  short helper of roughly \(30\)--\(60\) lines, mirroring one layer down the
  scheme-level \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) and with direct precedent
  in the analogous presheaf-pullback unfolding used for the base change of
  Kähler differentials); every other ingredient
  (\(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\),
  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\),
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\),
  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\)) is already present in Mathlib.
\end{proof}
input.new_string:
\emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) via
  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
  coherence isomorphisms
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.
\end{proof}

non-trivial fact about this operation that the group law needs is the
\emph{restriction-compatibility isomorphism}
\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}):
for \emph{arbitrary} modules \(M, N\) and the restriction along an open immersion

on all quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a
