\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}

The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
critical path.

\begin{definition}[\(\otimes\)-invertibility of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01HK (invertible modules) (verbatim text not yet
  % retrieved --- reference-retriever dispatch required before a verbatim
  % SOURCE QUOTE block may be added here).
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
  \[
    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
  \]
  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
  \(\otimes_X\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01CR (Picard group of a scheme) (verbatim text not yet
  % retrieved --- reference-retriever dispatch required before a verbatim
  % SOURCE QUOTE block may be added here).
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of objects of
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) under the substrate tensor product
  \(\otimes_X\) form a commutative monoid: multiplication is
  \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\), well-defined on isomorphism classes
  because \(\otimes_X\) is a bifunctor
  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, which transports
  \(M \cong M'\) to \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N\) and symmetrically);
  the unit is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and associativity, unitality and
  commutativity are the coherence isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, read as
  equalities of iso-classes. An element \([M]\) is a \emph{unit} of this monoid
  iff \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}): a tensor inverse \(N\) with
  \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) is exactly a multiplicative inverse of
  \([M]\). Hence the group of units of this commutative monoid is the abelian
  group of iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules ---
  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR). This mirrors Mathlib's
  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\)
  and the transported \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), used here as the design
  template; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at
  the pinned commit.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  Well-definedness of \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) on iso-classes is the
  bifunctoriality of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}. The
  commutative-monoid axioms are propositions about iso-classes and are
  discharged by the coherence isomorphisms: associativity by
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws by \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso},
  commutativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}; no pentagon, triangle or
  hexagon identity is invoked, since each axiom asserts only the
  \emph{existence} of an isomorphism. By definition of a unit in a commutative
  monoid, \([M]\) is invertible iff there is \([N]\) with
  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), i.e.\ iff \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). Therefore the units subgroup is
  precisely the iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible modules, and it is an
  abelian group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR). The whole
  assembly follows the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\)
  template.
\end{proof}

\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}

\emph{Step 3 (sectionwise identification along the open-immersion ring
  isomorphism).} The functor \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is
  \emph{defined} as the left adjoint of pushforward and is consequently
  \emph{opaque} on objects and morphisms: Mathlib provides no sectionwise
  formula for it, and --- unlike the scheme-level restriction --- no
  presheaf-level analogue of \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). The one
  genuinely-new project-side ingredient of this proof is therefore a
  \emph{sectionwise unfolding} of the abstract pullback along the open
  immersion: for the open-immersion ring morphism \(\varphi\), the presheaf
  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,P\) is, section by section over an
  open \(V\), the base change of the sections of \(P\) along the ring
  \emph{isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\) --- that is, restriction of
  sections combined with scalar transport across the isomorphism. (Over an open
  immersion the sections themselves agree definitionally,
  \(\Gamma(M|_f, V) = \Gamma(M, f(V))\) by the \(\mathtt{restrict\_obj}\)
  identity; only the scalar ring is transported, and it is transported along an
  isomorphism.)

  Granting this sectionwise description, the base-change comparison of Step 2
  is, on each section over \(V\), the canonical map of a tensor product under
  base change along the ring isomorphism \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Base change
  along a ring isomorphism is a \emph{monoidal equivalence} of module
  categories --- it is invertible and carries tensor products to tensor products
  --- by \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\) applied to
  the ring equivalence underlying \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V\). Hence each
  sectionwise comparison is an isomorphism compatible with \(\otimes\). No
  flatness, no \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\)-monoidality, and no strong-monoidal
  pullback instance is invoked: the entire force of the step is that the scalar
  change is along an \emph{isomorphism}.

  \emph{Gluing.} The sectionwise isomorphisms of Step 3 are natural in \(V\)
  (compatible with the presheaf restriction maps, the naturality square being
  that of \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) against restriction). They therefore assemble,
  via \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.isoMk}\), into a single presheaf-of-modules
  isomorphism realising the base-change comparison of Step 2 as an iso.
  Transporting this back through the sheafification units of Step 2 and the
  restriction--pullback comparison of Step 1 yields the asserted global
  isomorphism \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\).

  The route uses no monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) or on the
  pullback functor, no flatness, and no line-bundle hypothesis. The single
  bounded project-side ingredient is the sectionwise unfolding of
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) along an open immersion (a
  short helper of roughly \(30\)--\(60\) lines, mirroring one layer down the
  scheme-level \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) and with direct precedent
  in the analogous presheaf-pullback unfolding used for the base change of
  Kähler differentials); every other ingredient
  (\(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\),
  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\),
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\),
  \(\mathtt{isoMk}\)) is already present in Mathlib.
\end{proof}

566	\begin{lemma}
567	[Left and right unitors for $\otimes_X$]
568	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
569	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor, AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
570	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
571	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) an
572	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. With
573	  \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) the
574	  structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms
575	  \[
576	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M,
577	    \qquad
578	    M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M .
579	  \]
580	\end{lemma}
581	
582	\begin{proof}
583	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
584	  The presheaf monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) supplies
585	  the left and right unitors \(\lambda, \rho\) at the presheaf level. Their
586	  sheafifications \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\lambda)\),
587	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\rho)\), composed with the sheafification
588	  counit identifying the sheafified structure-sheaf presheaf with
589	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the step already used in the structure-sheaf-unit
590	  comparison \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) in the substrate file), give the
591	  two isomorphisms. As with the associator the route is the cheap
592	  \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern and uses no abstract pullback.
593	\end{proof}

595	\begin{lemma}
596	[Braiding for $\otimes_X$]
597	  \label{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_braiding}
599	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
600	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
601	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. There is a natural isomorphism
602	  \[
603	    M \otimes_X N \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; N \otimes_X M .
604	  \]
605	\end{lemma}
606	
607	\begin{proof}
608	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
609	  The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category is symmetric, so it carries a
610	  braiding \(\beta\) at the presheaf level
611	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}). Its
612	  sheafification \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\beta)\) is the asserted
613	  isomorphism, again by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern.
614	\end{proof}

616	\begin{lemma}
617	
618	\leanok
619	[Inverse of an invertible module]
620	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
621	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
622	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
623	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
624	  % NOTE: off the critical path for the relative Picard group law. The group
625	  % law uses the tensor-invertibility predicate IsInvertible
626	  % (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible), which carries the inverse existentially,
627	  % so no separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is required. Retained only
628	  % as a supplement on the geometric line-bundle subcategory.
629	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
630	  bundle. The dual
631	  \[
632	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
633	  \]
634	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
635	  isomorphism
636	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
637	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
638	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
639	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
640	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
641	\end{lemma}
642	
643	\begin{proof}
644	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
645	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
646	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
647	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
648	  The contraction map
649	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
650	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
651	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
652	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
653	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
654	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
655	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
656	\end{proof}

766	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
767	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
768	
769	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
770	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
771	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
772	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
773	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
774	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
775	design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
776	itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
777	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
778	critical path.
779	
780	\begin{definition}[\(\otimes\)-invertibility of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
781	  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
782	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
783	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
784	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01HK (invertible modules) (verbatim text not yet
785	  % retrieved --- reference-retriever dispatch required before a verbatim
786	  % SOURCE QUOTE block may be added here).
787	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
788	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
789	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
790	  \[
791	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
792	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
793	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
794	  \]
795	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
796	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
797	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
798	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
799	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
800	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
801	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
802	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
803	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
804	  \(\otimes_X\).
805	\end{definition}
806	
807	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
808	  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
809	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
810	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
811	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
812	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
813	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01CR (Picard group of a scheme) (verbatim text not yet
814	  % retrieved --- reference-retriever dispatch required before a verbatim
815	  % SOURCE QUOTE block may be added here).
816	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of objects of
817	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) under the substrate tensor product
818	  \(\otimes_X\) form a commutative monoid: multiplication is
819	  \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\), well-defined on isomorphism classes
820	  because \(\otimes_X\) is a bifunctor
821	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, which transports
822	  \(M \cong M'\) to \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N\) and symmetrically);
823	  the unit is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and associativity, unitality and
824	  commutativity are the coherence isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
825	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, read as
826	  equalities of iso-classes. An element \([M]\) is a \emph{unit} of this monoid
827	  iff \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
828	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}): a tensor inverse \(N\) with
829	  \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) is exactly a multiplicative inverse of
830	  \([M]\). Hence the group of units of this commutative monoid is the abelian
831	  group of iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules ---
832	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR). This mirrors Mathlib's
833	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\)
834	  and the transported \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\), used here as the design
835	  template; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at
836	  the pinned commit.
837	\end{lemma}
838	
839	\begin{proof}
840	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
841	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
842	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
843	  Well-definedness of \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) on iso-classes is the
844	  bifunctoriality of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}. The
845	  commutative-monoid axioms are propositions about iso-classes and are
846	  discharged by the coherence isomorphisms: associativity by
847	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws by \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso},
848	  commutativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}; no pentagon, triangle or
849	  hexagon identity is invoked, since each axiom asserts only the
850	  \emph{existence} of an isomorphism. By definition of a unit in a commutative
851	  monoid, \([M]\) is invertible iff there is \([N]\) with
852	  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), i.e.\ iff \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
853	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). Therefore the units subgroup is
854	  precisely the iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible modules, and it is an
855	  abelian group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR). The whole
856	  assembly follows the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\)
857	  template.
858	\end{proof}
859	
860	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
861	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
862	
863	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
864	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
865	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
866	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
867	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
868	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
869	
870	\begin{theorem}
871	
872	\leanok
873	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
874	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}

882	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,

884	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
885	
886	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
887	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
888	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
889	  % groups)

295:  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
318:\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
532:[Associator for $\otimes_X$]
534:  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
625:  % law uses the tensor-invertibility predicate IsInvertible
677:  % re-instantiated as `{ M : (pullback πC πT).Modules // IsInvertible M }`
683:  % separate predicate, connected to `IsInvertible` only by an
766:\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
782:  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
795:  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
809:  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
852:  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), i.e.\ iff \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
971:  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
975:  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
976:  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1037:    \texttt{IsInvertible}, a \(\mathrm{Prop}\) carrying a tensor inverse
1085:  \item Piece 3a--3b restrict the four existence-of-iso lemmas of Piece 2 to

85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.

87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the structure-sheaf unit are present as
89	scheme-level objects. Rather than the geometric local-triviality route, the
90	group law is built on \(\otimes\)-\emph{invertibility}: an object is a line
91	object when it admits a tensor inverse, \(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong
92	\mathcal{O}_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), exactly Mathlib's
93	\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom. Under this predicate requirement (3)
94	becomes the three monoidal \emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) ---
95	associator, unitors and braiding --- each obtained cheaply as
96	\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the corresponding presheaf-level
97	isomorphism, while the inverse is carried by the predicate itself. These
98	assemble the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is
99	the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The geometric
100	compatibility isomorphism \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)
101	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any separate tensor-inverse lemma are
102	\emph{off} this critical path. The full symmetric monoidal category on
103	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is also \emph{not} required; see
104	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
105	
106	\section{Mathlib API survey}
107	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
108	
109	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
110	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
111	
112	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
113	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
114	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
115	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
116	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
117	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
118	sectionwise tensor product
119	\[
120	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
121	\]
122	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
123	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
124	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of

555	  the sheafification functor to \(\alpha\) --- the cheap pattern
556	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\alpha)\), exactly the shape of the existing
557	  structure-sheaf-unit comparison and of \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\) --- and
558	  composing with the comparison identifying iterated sheafified tensor products
559	  with the sheafification of the iterated presheaf tensor product yields the
560	  asserted isomorphism. The construction uses only the monoidal coherence data
561	  already present on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\); it invokes neither
562	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) nor any open-immersion
563	  restriction-compatibility.
564	\end{proof}
565	
566	\begin{lemma}
567	[Left and right unitors for $\otimes_X$]
568	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
569	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor, AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
570	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
571	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) an
572	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. With
573	  \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) the
574	  structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms
575	  \[
576	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M,
577	    \qquad
578	    M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M .
579	  \]
580	\end{lemma}
581

tspivot209

COMPLETE (mathematical rewrite) / INCOMPLETE (verbatim Stacks citations only).

The ⊗-invertibility rewrite mandated by the directive was implemented via 11
targeted, LaTeX-balanced edits to the single target chapter. The one residual
gap is the verbatim citation discipline for the two Stacks-derived facts
(tags 01CR, 01HK): those reference files are **not present locally**, so per the
blueprint-writer descriptor's documented fallback the new Stacks-derived blocks
carry `% SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 0xxx (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` markers
instead of fabricated `% SOURCE QUOTE:` blocks. No citation was written from
memory (hard rule respected). A `reference-retriever` dispatch + a short
follow-up writer pass is needed to insert the verbatim quotes (see Notes).

### Environment caveat (important)
This session ran under a severe, persistent tool-output buffering/cancellation
fault: `Bash`/`Read` outputs returned empty for long stretches and then flushed
in large delayed bursts, and one parallel batch that mixed an exit-1 `Bash` with
my edits was **cancelled wholesale** (so an earlier edit attempt did NOT apply —
I re-issued all edits as pure-`Edit` batches with no `Bash`, which cannot be
cross-cancelled). The 11 final edits were issued with `old_string`s copied
verbatim from a confirmed full read of the file, so they match and apply; but I
was unable to obtain a clean read-back confirmation this session because the
verification reads were still buffered when I finalized. `Edit` is
corruption-safe here: on a non-match it no-ops, and every `new_string` is
LaTeX-balanced as a replacement, so the chapter remains valid LaTeX whether or
not any individual edit matched. **Recommend the plan agent skim the chapter /
run the typeset once to confirm all 11 landed.**

855	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
